APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (cross-lingual embeddings and bilingual lexicon induction) with broad appeal, presents a simple practical iterative self-learning framework, and demonstrates strong empirical performance across standard datasets (Europarl/OPUS, SdeWaC, Common Crawl) and tasks (BLI, cross-lingual word similarity). Evaluation compares to relevant baselines (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al., BilBOWA, etc.), reports hyperparameters, and releases code, making it well suited to a top-tier NLP/ML audience. Efficiency considerations and focus on very low-resource settings (25-word seeds, numeral lists) further increase its relevance to the venue. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles an important cross-lingual embedding problem with a simple, empirically effective iterative self-learning method that dramatically reduces supervision (works with 25-word seeds or numerals) and matches/outperforms strong dictionary- and parallel-based baselines. The work includes practical efficiency solutions (normalization, orthogonal maps, submatrix similarity computation), thorough experiments across multiple corpora/language pairs and standard benchmarks, and provides code and hyperparameter details — all aligning with the expectations of the venue and my emphasis on low-resource, reproducible methods.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (cross-lingual embeddings and bilingual lexicon induction) with broad appeal, presents a simple practical iterative self-learning framework, and demonstrates strong empirical performance across standard datasets (Europarl/OPUS, SdeWaC, Common Crawl) and tasks (BLI, cross-lingual word similarity). Evaluation compares to relevant baselines (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al., BilBOWA, etc.), reports hyperparameters, and releases code, making it well suited to a top-tier NLP/ML audience. Efficiency considerations and focus on very low-resource settings (25-word seeds, numeral lists) further increase its relevance to the venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles an important cross-lingual embedding problem with a simple, empirically effective iterative self-learning method that dramatically reduces supervision (works with 25-word seeds or numerals) and matches/outperforms strong dictionary- and parallel-based baselines. The work includes practical efficiency solutions (normalization, orthogonal maps, submatrix similarity computation), thorough experiments across multiple corpora/language pairs and standard benchmarks, and provides code and hyperparameter details — all aligning with the expectations of the venue and my emphasis on low-resource, reproducible methods.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states the main contributions early, making the iterative self-learning framework easy to follow. Notation for the linear/orthogonal mapping and algorithmic steps appears consistent and the description of practical efficiency fixes (normalization, submatrix similarity computation) is clear. Figures/tables and reported hyperparameters support reproducibility and are informative, though the presentation would benefit from a clearer discussion/visualization of stopping criteria and isolated ablation of key algorithmic choices (orthogonality, normalization) to improve interpretability. Overall the writing is adequate with only minor clarity improvements needed. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow (intro → iterative method → experiments → conclusion), and its main contributions are stated up front (self-learning loop that works with very small seeds and practical efficiency tricks). Notation and methods are described consistently (orthogonal mapping, nearest-neighbor retrieval, submatrix similarity computation) and hyperparameters/code are reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and dataset/baseline comparisons appear informative, though parts of the efficiency discussion (memory/submatrix strategy and runtime tradeoffs) read a bit dense and could be streamlined for practitioners.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states the main contributions early, making the iterative self-learning framework easy to follow. Notation for the linear/orthogonal mapping and algorithmic steps appears consistent and the description of practical efficiency fixes (normalization, submatrix similarity computation) is clear. Figures/tables and reported hyperparameters support reproducibility and are informative, though the presentation would benefit from a clearer discussion/visualization of stopping criteria and isolated ablation of key algorithmic choices (orthogonality, normalization) to improve interpretability. Overall the writing is adequate with only minor clarity improvements needed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow (intro → iterative method → experiments → conclusion), and its main contributions are stated up front (self-learning loop that works with very small seeds and practical efficiency tricks). Notation and methods are described consistently (orthogonal mapping, nearest-neighbor retrieval, submatrix similarity computation) and hyperparameters/code are reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and dataset/baseline comparisons appear informative, though parts of the efficiency discussion (memory/submatrix strategy and runtime tradeoffs) read a bit dense and could be streamlined for practitioners.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper composes established techniques rather than introducing a radical new theory, but they also concur it delivers a meaningful, practical advance—notably demonstrating strong performance from very small seeds and offering concrete efficiency tactics. Given the empirical novelty and valuable low-resource engineering contributions that go beyond mere incremental tuning, a score slightly above midpoint is appropriate. The work is not a conceptual breakthrough, but it is a well-differentiated, practically useful extension deserving a 4/5.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes an iterative self-learning loop that alternates orthogonal linear mapping learned from a tiny seed dictionary with induced full dictionaries, which is a practical and effective synthesis of existing components (orthogonal Procrustes-style mapping, nearest-neighbor induction, normalization tricks) and useful engineering (submatrix similarity computation). However, the core ideas are largely compositional rather than conceptually new relative to prior bilingual-mapping and self-learning approaches, and there is no indication of a novel theoretical perspective or guarantees that would mark a higher originality rating. The contribution is a meaningful incremental advance—notable for low-resource practicality—but does not constitute a highly original methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a compact, practical iterative self-learning loop for bilingual mapping that—while building on existing linear-mapping and nearest-neighbour induction ideas—clearly differentiates itself by showing that very small seeds (25 words or numerals) suffice and by proposing concrete efficiency tactics (submatrix similarity computation, normalization/orthogonal constraints). From a low-resource engineering perspective this is a notable and useful novelty: it is not a radical theoretical breakthrough but a non-trivial, well-differentiated extension of prior dictionary-based mappings with strong empirical focus and reproducible code. An expert would be informed/surprised by the demonstrated low-seed effectiveness and the pragmatic efficiency contributions, so it merits above-incremental scoring.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against a wide and relevant set of baselines (both dictionary-based and parallel-corpus methods: Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Zhang et al., Artetxe et al., Lazaridou et al., Luong et al., BilBOWA, Upadhyay et al.), using standard datasets and metrics (bilingual lexicon induction accuracy and cross-lingual word similarity) and reporting hyperparameters and code to support reproducibility. Comparisons appear fair: same data splits (multiple public corpora and established test sets) and evaluation metrics are used, and the paper explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better. The authors analyze practical differences (orthogonal constraint, normalization, and scalable submatrix similarity computation) that distinguish their approach from baselines, and related work coverage is described as thorough. | B(5): The paper compares to a comprehensive set of relevant baselines (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al., Zhang et al., Lazaridou et al., Luong et al., BilBOWA, Upadhyay et al.), including both dictionary- and parallel-corpus-based methods. Comparisons appear fair: same publicly used datasets (Dinu et al., Europarl/OPUS splits, SdeWaC, Common Crawl), standard metrics (BLI accuracy, word-similarity correlation), hyperparameters reported and code released. The authors note when prior methods match or outperform theirs in some settings and analyze differences and practical trade-offs (efficiency, memory, iteration cost), with thorough related-work discussion—aligning well with reproducibility and low-resource concerns.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a wide and relevant set of baselines (both dictionary-based and parallel-corpus methods: Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Zhang et al., Artetxe et al., Lazaridou et al., Luong et al., BilBOWA, Upadhyay et al.), using standard datasets and metrics (bilingual lexicon induction accuracy and cross-lingual word similarity) and reporting hyperparameters and code to support reproducibility. Comparisons appear fair: same data splits (multiple public corpora and established test sets) and evaluation metrics are used, and the paper explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better. The authors analyze practical differences (orthogonal constraint, normalization, and scalable submatrix similarity computation) that distinguish their approach from baselines, and related work coverage is described as thorough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to a comprehensive set of relevant baselines (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al., Zhang et al., Lazaridou et al., Luong et al., BilBOWA, Upadhyay et al.), including both dictionary- and parallel-corpus-based methods. Comparisons appear fair: same publicly used datasets (Dinu et al., Europarl/OPUS splits, SdeWaC, Common Crawl), standard metrics (BLI accuracy, word-similarity correlation), hyperparameters reported and code released. The authors note when prior methods match or outperform theirs in some settings and analyze differences and practical trade-offs (efficiency, memory, iteration cost), with thorough related-work discussion—aligning well with reproducibility and low-resource concerns.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is strong, reproducible, and compares to many baselines, but is limited by missing theoretical analysis, some ablations (e.g., orthogonality/normalization/stopping criteria), and narrower typological coverage and deployment analyses. Given the solid experimental contribution and practical value despite those gaps, a score of 4 appropriately reflects strong empirical substance with room for broader analysis and theoretical grounding.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental suite is empirically thorough in comparing many baselines across several English–European language pairs and low-resource seed settings, with code and hyperparameters provided and practical efficiency tricks evaluated. However, from a theory-first perspective the paper lacks any formal analysis or guarantees for the iterative self-learning procedure, and there is insufficient ablation isolating core algorithmic choices (e.g. the effect of orthogonality, normalization, stopping criteria). Linguistic evaluation is concentrated on Indo‑European/Europarl-type corpora, limiting claims about broad low-resource applicability. The authors do acknowledge practical limitations (runtime, memory) and provide reproducibility details, but the missing theoretical grounding and limited typological coverage justify a moderate score.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the experimental suite is strong for low-resource claims: evaluations span multiple corpora (SdeWaC, Common Crawl variants, Europarl), standard lexicon and similarity benchmarks, and comparisons to a wide set of baselines, including both dictionary- and parallel-based methods. The paper includes sensible sensitivity checks (seed sizes down to 25 words and numeral-only seeds), reports hyperparameters and releases code, and addresses practical efficiency (orthogonality, normalization, submatrix similarity computation). I dock one point because some practical analyses are missing or limited for deployment: more languages/typologies, explicit per-iteration runtime/memory trade-offs, and experiments with non-linear mappings or alternative induction strategies would strengthen claims of generality.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a strong, practical contribution by enabling lexicon induction with extremely small supervision and providing tractable, well-engineered methods and public code that practitioners can adopt. It is primarily an empirical and procedural advance rather than a theoretical breakthrough—lacking formal analysis of convergence or optimality—which limits its impact on theory but not its clear value for applied low-resource cross-lingual work. Given the robustness, practicality, and likely adoption, a score of 4 reflects its substantive applied impact without overstating theoretical novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—reducing bilingual supervision for lexicon induction—and demonstrates strong empirical gains in low-resource settings (working from 25-word seeds or numeral lists) along with useful engineering (normalization, orthogonal mapping, submatrix similarity computation). However, from a theory-first perspective its contribution is primarily empirical and procedural (iterative self-learning of linear mappings) with no formal analysis of convergence, optimality, or conditions for success, and it confines itself to linear dictionary-based mappings. These limitations reduce its potential to fundamentally shift theoretical understanding, although the pragmatic results and available code make it likely to be adopted and extended in applied work.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important practical problem—bilingual lexicon induction for low-resource languages—by showing a simple iterative self-learning loop can work with extremely small supervision (as few as 25 seed words or numeral lists) and matches or outperforms several dictionary- and corpus-based baselines. The emphasis on tractable implementation (orthogonal mappings, length normalization, and submatrix similarity computations), public code, and detailed hyperparameter reporting makes the method readily adoptable in resource-constrained settings, so practitioners will likely build on it. While not a theoretical breakthrough, the demonstrated robustness and practicality across several real corpora and evaluation sets open realistic avenues for low-resource cross-lingual work and incremental improvements, so I view the contribution as strong but not transformative.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper’s claims are clearly stated and well supported by empirical results across multiple datasets and competitive baselines, with correct application of standard mathematical components and practical implementation details. I dock one point because Reviewer A rightly notes the absence of formal theoretical analysis of the iterative self-learning loop and limited ablation/typological diversity, which weakens the paper’s completeness though not its empirical validity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with empirical evidence across several datasets and baselines (e.g. EN–IT, EN–DE, Europarl, Common Crawl), showing strong low-resource performance. However, from the summary there is no formal theoretical analysis or convergence proof for the iterative self-learning loop (only orthogonal mapping and normalization heuristics), which is a key omission given my theory-first criteria. It is also unclear whether core design choices (orthogonality constraint, normalization, stopping criteria, submatrix similarity computation) were isolated in ablation studies, and the language evaluation concentrates on similar Indo‑European pairs limiting typological validation. Overall the experimental methodology appears sound, but the lack of rigorous theoretical justification and comprehensive ablations prevents a higher technical score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s primary claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: repeated orthogonal mapping plus induced-dictionary self-learning is evaluated across diverse datasets and competitive baselines (including both dictionary- and parallel-corpus-based methods) and shown to work with very small seeds (25 words or numerals). Mathematical pieces (orthogonal mapping, nearest-neighbor retrieval) are standard and appear correctly applied, and practical efficiency concerns are acknowledged and addressed with concrete submatrix/vectorized strategies. Experimental methodology is sound—proper baselines, multiple datasets, reported hyperparameters and released code—so there are no obvious logical gaps that would undermine the conclusions, aside from the expected trade-off of increased total runtime which the paper openly discusses.

main_claims: Introduce a simple iterative self-learning framework that repeatedly learns an embedding mapping from a seed bilingual dictionary, induces a full dictionary, and uses that induced dictionary to relearn an improved mapping until convergence.

The proposed self-learning approach greatly reduces the amount of bilingual supervision required, working with as little as a 25-word seed dictionary or an automatically generated numeral list while obtaining competitive results.

The method outperforms or matches several prior dictionary-based and parallel-corpus-based baselines on bilingual lexicon induction and cross-lingual word similarity, especially in low-resource (small seed dictionary) settings.

Efficiency considerations are crucial; the paper proposes practical solutions (length normalization/mean centering, orthogonal mapping, vectorized submatrix computation of similarity matrices) to make the iterative framework tractable.
_summary.methodology: Iteratively: (1) learn an orthogonal linear mapping W from source to target embeddings using a seed binary dictionary D; (2) induce a full bilingual dictionary by nearest-neighbor retrieval (dot product / cosine after normalization) from mapped source embeddings to target embeddings; (3) replace D with the induced dictionary and repeat until convergence. Similarity matrices are computed in vectorized submatrices for memory efficiency.
datasets: English–Italian public dataset by Dinu et al. (2015)
Europarl-derived dictionaries from OPUS (used to create training/test sets)
Common Crawl corpora used at WMT 2016 (8 billion and 2.8 billion variants)
limitations: Iterative loop increases total training time proportional to number of iterations.
Computing the full similarity matrix XWZ^T can be too large to fit in memory for large vocabularies, requiring submatrix computation strategies.
The paper focuses on dictionary-based linear mappings; non-linear transformations and alternative dictionary induction methods are left for future work (stated as future directions).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - bilingual/cross-lingual word embeddings and bilingual lexicon induction

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in machine learning theory and algorithmic foundations with a track record of publishing formal analyses of representation learning and optimization.
traits: Penalizes empirical papers that lack theoretical guarantees or formal analysis of convergence/optimality (will deduct points if no proof or rigorous argument is provided).
Requires clear algorithmic novelty beyond simple composition of existing techniques (will score down if contribution is primarily an application of known methods without new theoretical insight).

Demands comprehensive ablation of core algorithmic choices (will penalize if key design decisions—e.g., orthogonality constraint, normalization steps, iterative stopping criteria—are not isolated and analyzed).
Insists on linguistically diverse evaluation (will deduct points if experiments concentrate on similar Indo-European languages or few language typologies).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critical and formal: focuses on theory, clarity of algorithmic contribution, and whether empirical evidence is matched by principled analysis.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Low-Resource Engineer
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher/engineer focused on low-resource language technologies, reproducible systems, and efficient algorithms for deployment in resource-constrained settings.
traits: Rewards simplicity and empirical effectiveness, especially methods that achieve strong results with minimal supervision (will give credit for working with very small seed dictionaries or automatic heuristics like numerals).
Values runtime/memory efficiency and practical implementation details (will score higher if paper provides concrete strategies for making methods tractable and includes timing/memory comparisons).

Prefers reproducibility and practical baselines (will reward papers that compare against both dictionary-based and parallel-corpus-based baselines and provide clear experimental protocols and seed-size scaling experiments).
Favors methods that generalize across many realistic low-resource scenarios (will score up if experiments include diverse low-resource language settings and realistic seed construction procedures).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and empirical: emphasizes real-world utility, simplicity, efficiency, and strong low-resource experimental validation over formal proofs.
